one_line_takeaway: Self-Sanitize adds a lightweight hidden-representation classifier that watches an LLM's own token stream for privacy-leaking intent and interrupts/rewrites in-place mid-response, cutting privacy-leakage scores far below 11 baselines (e.g., averaged UAL top-1 leakage accuracy 3.00 vs. 45.00 undefended) while adding only ~2% generation-time overhead and ~8% first-token latency, with negligible MT-Bench/ICL utility loss (7.04/41.96 vs. 7.05/43.36 undefended).
key_finding: Self-Monitor achieves ~97% detection accuracy across the three leakage scenarios and four LLMs, and typically triggers within about 10 tokens (Figure 3; LLaMA-2 requires more tokens, attributed to its verbose flattery prefacing responses). In Table 1 (harmful/leakage scores, lower is better, averaged across 4 LLMs), Self-Sanitize attains the lowest average score in all three scenarios: PDL 7.88 vs. next-best baseline Self-Defend's 10.37 and No-Defense's 48.29; UAL 3.00 vs. Self-Defense's 22.08/Self-Defend's 7.50 vs. No-Defense's 45.00; PCL 1.67 vs. Self-Defend's 2.08 vs. No-Defense's 28.78. The paper reports a maximum 83.68% reduction in malicious-query performance for PDL and a maximum 95.72% reduction in top-1 accuracy for UAL, and states all baselines achieve at least a 50.71% decline in PCL recovery accuracy (i.e., PCL is comparatively easy to mitigate for every method). On utility (Table 2), Self-Sanitize scores MT-Bench 7.04 / ICL 41.96 versus No-Defense's 7.05 / 43.36, a negligible drop, while LLM-based and instruction-based baselines show roughly 17.4% and 5.8% MT-Bench score declines respectively (paper's own stated figures). On overhead (Figure 4), Self-Sanitize shows only ~2% increase in ATGR and ~8% increase in ATLR relative to no defense, contrasted with other LLM-based baselines that either generate substantially more tokens or incur much higher latency.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Self-Sanitize, an LLM-driven mitigation framework with two novel modules: (1) Self-Monitor, a lightweight hierarchical classifier that reads intermediate hidden representations of the LLM at each generated token (via a representation hook at a chosen layer, following representation-engineering work) to detect harmful/privacy-leaking intent in real time, using a coarse-grained (safe/harmful) classifier plus a fine-grained harm-type classifier, smoothed by a 'consistency monitor window' to reduce false positives, and paired with a 'regurgitant cache' that withholds the last m tokens from the user so they can be rewound if an interrupt fires; and (2) Self-Repair, an in-place repair mechanism that, upon interrupt, freezes the already-streamed response as a fixed prefix and forces the LLM to continue generation conditioned on a harm-type-specific repair prompt within the same dialogue turn, avoiding the need to re-run the whole conversation through a separate review dialogue. The explicit motivation (Section 4.1) is that prior post-hoc/shadow-LLM defenses are incompatible with token-level streaming output and add high latency/memory overhead; Self-Sanitize is designed to operate concurrently with generation and interrupt/patch mid-stream.
threat_model: Defined formally in Section 3.2: a malicious user with query-only (black-box chat/completion API) access sends an adversarial prompt Q_adv to maximize a harmfulness scorer S_harm over the LLM's response; the paper scopes 'harmful' specifically to privacy-violating content generation (revealing sensitive attributes about individuals/organizations), not toxicity/bias/illegal content broadly. The defense's goal is formalized as an inf-sup optimization: minimize S_harm(M_theta(Q_adv | D)) subject to a bounded drop in helpfulness S_help on benign/regular prompts Q_reg (utility constraint beta). No white-box or weight-poisoning attacker is considered; the adversary only crafts prompts.
limitations: The paper's own text flags several caveats: (1) LLaMA-2 needs more tokens before triggering self-repair due to verbose preambles, i.e., detection latency is model-dependent; (2) in the UAL scenario, Self-Sanitize's mitigation does NOT improve with more capable LLMs (unlike PDL) because more intelligent models infer user attributes more accurately, increasing the underlying leakage risk the defense must counter; (3) the paper explicitly invokes a 'no-free-lunch' argument that privacy mitigation cannot be achieved without some utility cost, motivating their MT-Bench utility check rather than claiming zero cost; (4) the threat model is restricted to a black-box, query-only adversary and to privacy-leakage harms specifically (not general toxicity/jailbreak content), so generalization to other harm categories or white-box/weight-level attacks is not evaluated in the main text (from the excerpted first ~22 pages). The paper does not report false-positive-rate figures on purely benign queries for the harmfulness classifier itself beyond the MT-Bench/ICL utility proxy, and the representation-hook classifier requires per-model training data (implied by 'hierarchical classifier' training, though the labeled training procedure/dataset for the monitor classifier itself was not located within the pages reviewed).

datasets_used: PFL-DocVAQ (Private Demonstrations Leakage / PDL scenario, following Wu et al. 2024 setup)
Synthetic Reddit-style comments dataset from Yukhymenko et al. 2024 (User Attributes Leakage / UAL scenario)
DecodingTrust conversation-leakage subset (Wang et al. 2023) (Previous Conversation Leakage / PCL scenario)
MT-Bench (Zheng et al. 2023) for multi-turn utility evaluation
quantitative_results: Self-Monitor detection accuracy ~97% across 3 scenarios x 4 LLMs (Figure 3a); triggers within ~10 tokens for most LLMs (Figure 3b)

Table 1 average harmful/leakage scores across 4 LLMs (lower=better): PDL - No Defense 48.29, Self-Defend 10.37, Self-Sanitize 7.88; UAL - No Defense 45.00, GPT-4 AA 18.17, Self-Defense 22.08, Self-Sanitize 3.00; PCL - No Defense 28.78, Self-Defend 2.08, Self-Sanitize 1.67
Maximum 83.68% reduction in malicious-query performance (PDL scenario) and maximum 95.72% reduction in top-1 attribute-inference accuracy (UAL scenario), stated by paper
All baselines achieve at least a 50.71% decline in PCL recovery accuracy versus No Defense (paper's own claim)

Table 2 utility: Self-Sanitize MT-Bench 7.04 / ICL 41.96 vs. No Defense 7.05 / 43.36; LLM-based baselines ~17.4% MT-Bench decrease, instruction-based baselines ~5.8% MT-Bench decrease (paper-stated aggregate figures)

Figure 4 overhead: Self-Sanitize ATGR increase ~2%, ATLR (first-token latency) increase ~8% relative to No Defense; other LLM-based baselines (e.g., Self-Defend, Self-Evaluation) show substantially higher ATGR/ATNR/ATLR ratios (up to ~2.0-2.1x in some cases per Figure 4 values)
baselines_compared: GPT-4 AA (GPT-4-based adversarial anonymization, upper-bound reference for UAL only, Staab et al. 2024)
